words: This conversation with IDA MIELKE NEWMAN TOOK PLACE at her home at Cameron on February 18,1977. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.

speaker: SS
words: When did your people first come here?

speaker: IDA MIELKE NEWMAN
words: They came here in 1901. I was three years old.

speaker: SS
words: And where was it they came from?

speaker: IMN
words: Minnesota. Oh, there were quite a number of families came here from Minnesota. Reverend Hopf was a minister- was their minister in Minnesota and he moved to Cameron and he told congre gation what a wonderful country it was. So I don't Inow how many families- seven or eight families came through him. They were mem bers of his congregation.

speaker: SS
words: Where in Minnesota?

speaker: IMN
words: From Arlington and New Auburn.

speaker: SS
words: Were your parents born in Germany?

speaker: IMN
words: My mother was born in Germany and my father was born in Minnesota. My mother was four years old when her parents came across.

speaker: SS
words: So then your fa^fcer and mother both probably spoke English pretty well?

speaker: IMN
words: My dad did; my mother spoken broken English, because in Minnesota where they lived they were all German, everyone talked German. And even here when I was a youngster everyone talked German. I couldn't talk English when I started to school. Are there other things you'd like to know?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, yes. I'm just getting started really. When they came, your parents, did they have much?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, no, people were very poor. They had I think, I think my dad bought 160 acres, and I don't know just what he paid down whether it was$1,000 or $2,000, and that's all they had. And, of course, they brought along part of their goods.

speaker: IMN
words: Did they have much of an outfit to bring?

speaker: IMN
words: No, as far as machinery is concerned, no. Just their clothes and I think they brought oh, some of their household goods.

speaker: IMN
words: Did they come with other families at the same time?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. My grandparents came at the same time, and I don't know just what other families. And then some other families came the next year, in 1902. The Hardungs and the Mielkes came in 1901 and the Wagners and the Blicks and Bront - I was trying to think of some more names- came the next year.

speaker: IMN
words: When you came were you old enough to remember that at all?

speaker: IMN
words: I remember very, very little. See I was only four- I wasn't even four, I was only three. And I don t know I remember it or whether I just remember from hearsay, you know, a great many times they'll talk about it and you just think that you remember.

speaker: IMN
words: Right. Did they say it was rough at all? Or was it an easy- they took the train out here.

speaker: IMN
words: They took the train and my mother always said when she came to Kendrick, she says, "Oh, my goodness, how in the world are we ever going to get out of this hole?" See, Minnesota everything was level, and she didn't they'd ever get out of this. She said, "There can't be any farming in country like this." (Laughter) It probably was a disappointment then. Well, she wasn't really disappointed, because she knew my dad wouldn't buy something that he couldn't take care of.

speaker: IMN
words: Was he out here before she?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Yes, he was out here and bought the farm before she came out.

speaker: IMN
words: How many brothers and sisters?

speaker: IMN
words: Let's see, there was seven of us. There were three brothers born in Minnesota and my sister and I were born in Minnesota and then two brothers were born in Cameron. And the place that my father bought was the old Cameron place, Cameron was named after. See, Mr. Cameron had the first post office and he was quite a prominient man and so when he was county commissioner at Lewiston-

speaker: SS
words: Was he German, too? Didn't sound like it.

speaker: IMN
words: No, he wasn't German.

speaker: SS
words: Did the community start off then not as a German community?

speaker: IMN
words: No, it didn't start as a German community.

speaker: SS
words: It became that way-

speaker: IMN
words: Tt became that way through the church, I think. The church was built and through the church, the Lutheran Church, the people came and set tled here. It was mostly theouth that that they settled right around Cameron.

speaker: SS
words: Did the church draw on a big area, do you think, to start with?

speaker: IMN
words: No, just Cameron, just, Cameron, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was Hopf the first minister?

speaker: IMN
words: No. It's in the book who the first minister-

speaker: SS
words: I know Hattie Johnson told me the first minister was from Germany, he and his wife.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And she had a difficult time adapting, she said, to the country.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, I see.

speaker: SS
words: To get used to it. When you were old enough to really remembering things, what was it like around here when you were a kid?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, there were lots and lots of trees, but the contour of the coun try is different because people had to make a living, you couldn't go to the store and buy things, just, you know, your very necessities And you have to have your own cattle, and your own fruit and your garden. You raised your own things, your chickens and your pigs that you butchered in the fall, and cured your meat and that was your meat supply for the year, and your chickens. And you canned your fruit and you made sauerkraut and you had your potatoes and apples. So many apples that you stored in the winter. And made your own butter.

speaker: SS
words: Was it densely settled then?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, much more so than now. The farms were, oh, forty acres, eighty acres and 160 acres was a fairly good sized farm. And the people farmed with horses; usually had four horses and a footburner and seeder and a harrow. You know, footburner, just a hookon plow and that's about all you had.

speaker: SS
words: How much neighboring? Was there a lot?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, there was a lot of neighboring. People visited back and forth. You go over to some people's place on Sunday for dinner and you eat what they had, and you were very welcome. And people visi ted back and forth a lot. And in the evenings, a lot of times, get together and play cards or tell stories. You know, stories handed down fuorn generation to generation.

speaker: SS
words: Stories originating in the old country?

speaker: IMN
words: From the old country, yes. And then, of course, my parents tell stories from Minnesota.

speaker: SS
words: When you say they were handed down; I don't expect you remember the stories themselves, but what kind of things would they be likely to be about?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, one thing that I remember- I don't remember the story itself, but my grandmother, she was born in 1827, she lived with us because grandfather had passed away, was telling about spooks. (Chuckles) Of course, things we don't believe in now, but- she didn't be lieve in it either but she was telling about it and oh, we thought those stories were wonderful! And then they used to tell about the different people, about different things that they did.

speaker: SS
words: You don't remember what she had to say about spooks-

speaker: IMN
words: No, I wish to goodness I could.

speaker: SS
words: I'll bet that would be fun.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. And when she was in Germany they were in Prussia, they were near the Polish border, and I remember her teaching me some Polish and oh, I wish I could remember it! I can remember-(Polish) one, two, three more Polish. And I wish I could remember more.

speaker: SS
words: There was an awful lot of German spoken here at that time.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was most of the talking in German?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, the talking was all in German. And, oh, I don't really know when they started to talk English, I suppose when the children had to go to school. h After I was older we had dances, we had dances and we'd have parties, and on Friday we'd go over to somebody's house in the afternoon, young people'd meet, and we'd talk and and we just had a lot of fun. And the celebrations when I was a youngster were the Fourth of July- they'd meet at some- oh, in some grove or in some people's pasture, we had nice green pastures and play cards- not play cards- but play games, baseball and races.

speaker: SS
words: Did you play- were there outdoor games that you used to play like circle games and that kind of stuff?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, yeah, we used to play Drop-The-Handkerchief, then in the win ter Fox-and-Goose, and I was trying to think of some other games. Oh, there were different ' that we played, in addition to base ball.

speaker: SS
words: You say dances? Dancing was quite acceptable, nobody objected to having dances around here?

speaker: SS
words: Some places I know they did.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. No, they didn't. Of course, there were certain people that it was against their religion and so on didn't go to dances, but most of 'em did. And it was of course, like in Cameron, when we had the dances, it was just a whole group, of course,they had some At that s about two miles away, that's about as far away we got.

speaker: SS
words: Dances at homes or in the school?

speaker: IMN
words: No, in the homes. At first we had 'em in the homes then after while a hall at Cameron, used to be the old post office, we used to have 'em in there.

speaker: SS
words: Be the whole neighborhood or just a few neighbors?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, it was in the neighborhood then a few of the young people around And then another thing we had when I went to school was Literary Soc ieties. And they were a lot of fun. Had debates and oh, recitations and singing. And one of the things that was a lot of fun- they had a paper called the "Hog's Eye" and it would be some stories made up of people in the neighborhood. We always thought that was a lot of fun.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder why the "Hog's Eye"? That's kind of an amusing name for it.

speaker: IMN
words: It was supposed to he amusing, it was for fun.

speaker: SS
words: Joke.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Would the stories be true, or would they be—

speaker: IMN
words: No, no, they wouldn't be true. No, they were just for fun.

speaker: SS
words: When you had say, like debates, would that be taking two sides of a question?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Would,they be serious?

speaker: IMN
words: They were real serious debates. I remember one of the jokes that the "Hog's Eye"- one of the menat the table- someone asked one of the children in school, "What does your father do when you sit down to eat before you take your food?" "Well," he says, "my father says, "go easy with the butter, kids, fifteen cents a pound!" (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: It's supposed to be grace, right?

speaker: IMN
words: It's supposed to be grace, but that s what the youngster's father- the first thing he said! (Chuckles) It's a little different than now, the price.

speaker: SS
words: What about the debates? Do you remember any of the sort of thing that- you say serious- the sort of thing the people would talk about? Was it politics or religion?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, no, it'd be politics and different subjects- wish I could re- member some of I don't. I know when I went to school and the first debate I ever was in. I don't know what grade I was in, it must have been about the sixth- fifth or sixth- and the topic was "Resolved that having the printing press was much more important than the discovery of America." And I was on the printing press side, And my teacher was printing the paper, so of course, I thought I had a real, real good point, so I says, "Well, if the printing press hadn't of been founded, my teacher couldn't have printed the paper." And when the opponent got up he says, "No, he couldn't have printed this paper, but the paper isn't any good anyway!" So that was the end of our debate!

speaker: SS
words: The end of all the debates?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, the teacher didn't let us have any more debates! (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: Because of that?

speaker: IMN
words: I don't know, I imagine so. If we did have, I don't remember.

speaker: SS
words: Did he put out the Cameron paper? Did he make any money, you think, doing that?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, not too much, sort of on the side.

speaker: SS
words: Was that paper in German at the time?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, no. That was english. That was when we were going to English school.

speaker: SS
words: Was the paper for the whole community?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, whoever subscribed for it, just the same as any other paper. It was just news from Cameron and surrounding territory Just a little pamphlet.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I'm real curious about some of the customs when you were youngthat people still kept that were from the old country- the sort of things that have died out since then.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, the celebration of Christmas, and giving Christmas dinners and family together at Christmas at one of the- I think was handed down.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a family or was it a community celebration?

speaker: IMN
words: Family. That was a family celebration. Bee, I was trying to think of some community celebrations. We used to have picnics. I remember. And then one of the biggest things was on Christmas Eve, to go to church and the children would have new dresses and new clothes, and that was on« of the big things in the community.

speaker: SS
words: You would go to church Christmas Eve? Was there a celebration at church besides the service?

speaker: IMN
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: That was all at home.

speaker: IMN
words: That was all- yes. We didn't have any celebrations then, we have nowmore so now than we used to years ago.

speaker: SS
words: I know Frank Brocke and you probably know him-

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. I know him.

speaker: SS
words: He told me how his father used to always dress up as Santa Claus and come in and the kids were always scared of him, they were always sure he was Santa Claus even they knew he was their father, they couldn't recognize him.

speaker: IMN
words: we always got some gift for Christmas, but never Santa Clausbecause Santa Claus would come in a sleigh, and of course, there was always one little thing- but we didn't get very many things- people just couldn't afford it. And, of course, the next morning, we'd say, "Yeah, we heard Santa Claus." And one thing we always did, we always decorated the tree; everyone did and they made a lot of their decorations. Once in a while we make 'em now to show people what kind of decorations we used to have. Then Easter- there's another thing they'd celebrate, you know, Easter Day. We'd havewe didn't have eggs all winter- saved up a few eggs, so we could have some for Easter. And I was just trying to think. the Fourth of July celebrations- was always celebrated.

speaker: SS
words: That would have to be an American one.

speaker: IMN
words: That's an American one. And I was just thinking what other holidays there were- those I think vrere the traditional holidays.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard about there was low German and a High German in the dialect.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, and I can talk both of 'em. My dad and his mother talked Low German and we children talked Low German to them, and my mother didn't talk Low German, she talked the regular German-High German and we talked High German to her.

speaker: SS
words: Now what was the difference? I mean,why were there two different ones? Depends on where you come from?

speaker: IMN
words: Well,no, they had different dialects in Germany the same as they have here, if you're from the South or from the North or from the West your dialects are a little bit different.

speaker: SS
words: Why did they call the one Low and the other one High?

speaker: IMN
words: I don't know why it was called Low- but the Low German is not a German, the High German- the regular German is, German and the Low German is not the German.

speaker: SS
words: The regular German is that what they use in the church?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a lot of difference between 'em?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, yes, there's a lot of difference. I was just trying to think- Maybe if you'd give me a word, maybe I could tell oou maybe I could tell you whether it was in High German or Low German.

speaker: SS
words: Well, what about something like school?

speaker: IMN
words: School? Shull That would be in German- ?

speaker: SS
words: Or like church?

speaker: IMN
words: Church? (can't spell the German) And they didn't have it in the High German.

speaker: SS
words: Well, could people who could only speak one or the other, could they speak to each other easily and make themselves understood?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Yes I think they could pretty well understand one another.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there was much desire to become Americanized here?

speaker: IMN
words: Very, very much so...My Grandfather came from Germany- he became an American citizen right away because he wanted to be an American citi zen. And of course, then, the Civil War broke out and he was in the Civil War in Sherman's March to the Sea. And my folks always taught us to be very good Americans, that's what they came to this country for. And that's why we had these Fourth of July celebrations- well there was a lot, a lot of patriotism shown. Always had a speaker to talk about the good of this country and different things. And then they usually eneded up - the program- with singing America and every one sang and really meant it.

speaker: SS
words: But, say like, from day-to-day though, since this was mostly all German community right here- so people probably didn't have too much contact with people that weren't German, at least when they were right here.

speaker: IMN
words: Not right at Cameron, but if they did their trading at Kendrick and that was English- so it was just community right in itself was German, but as far as trading with Lewiston and so on. And I know in- when the First World War broke out they sold more War Bonds they really supported their country.

speaker: SS
words: That's funny, because I know I've heard that some people did- Frank Brocke talked to me and other people, too, who were German and they have told me that people got a hard time because they were German during that time. Some people gave 'em a hard time.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, there were some people. That's one reason my mother never liked to talk much when she was out. She spoke broken English, She had the accent, and it always bothered her. And nowdays if she would be talking now-

speaker: SS
words: But at that time, I've heard- I heard that especially like Leland there were people in Leland that really gave Cameron a hard time during that time.

speaker: IMN
words: I can tell you something now- something that happened after the First World War and I'm not going to mention any names because I wouldn't want any names being mentioned- someone tore down- the flag schoolhouse waan't taken down for some reason or other at night and somebody came there and took the flag and stomped it down into the ground, to give the German people a bad name. Of course, that person has died or moved away from here a long time ago, so I hope that I don't hurt any feelings, I just loved the people. And there was someone at the schoolhouse that night and saw this.

speaker: SS
words: Saw it done?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, and they weren't supposed to be there so they couldn't say any thing. Of course, I knew it at the time, but I never said anything because I wasn't supposed to say anything, and I wasn't going to tell and get someone into trouble. So those things did happen.

speaker: SS
words: Were they there on or something like that?

speaker: IMN
words: At night; that's it. They were there on date. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: I think it was Hattie Johnson that told me that- now, I don't know if you remember this happening, but she said that when boys were going off to war at one point, there was a minister from Kendrick that really gave them a hard time. You remember that?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I don't, although her brother went at the same time my brothers went. Now, it could be.

speaker: SS
words: She said, I think she said that he gave a farewell dinner and he said some things like, you know- "If I had any German blood, I would get rid of it." You know. Real patriotic.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Now, we were always taught, ray mother always taught us that it doesn't make any difference what color you are, or what creed, or what nationality, or how much education you have or how little edu cation you have, it doesn't make any difference, it's what you do with yourself and do with the rest of the people. That's what counts. And so, we always tried to live by that.

speaker: SS
words: When I heard what I heard, it seemed very unfair.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, there were incidents; I don't know of any incident right now, but . do know even in World War II- now all of my children were inboys were in- one of 'em was a German prisoner, he was shot down over? and another one was in Patton's Army and one boy was in Korea and the daughter took her nurse's training through the services. They were all in it, I know that before the second boy went, he was ahead of his age in school,,they were wondering why he wasn't in the army and he was only seventeen years old. So, I know that those things happen.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds like the business of War Bonds- I heard- what I've heard elsewhere from people who weren't German, that they expected German people to buy more than anybody else to prove that they were on the right side.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. And these people did buy a lot of War Bonds. And there may have been one or two families that were a little prejudiced, I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: Well, they still had ties to the old country.

speaker: IMN
words: We still have some relatives there.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know why- why people were leaving Germany at the time and coming over? Why they figured there was a better chance here?

speaker: IMN
words: My Grandfather 'the Mielke side came over because he did not like all these wars. He saw so much fighting there. So that's one reason he came here. And he's the one that served in the Civil War. And then my grandfather Hartung my mother's side, see there was a class distinction in the old country. And my grandmother was from the upper class, from well educated people and my grandfather was more of a common person, and ofcourse, that was looked down upon.

speaker: SS
words: But he married your grandmother anyway?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. He was a wonderful man and a wonderful broadminded man. To tell the truth, moreso than my grandmother.

speaker: SS
words: Maybe because he was common instead of high.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, I think that had something to do with it. And that's one reason they came to the United States. America.

speaker: SS
words: Because of the class distinction?

speaker: IMN
words: Because of the class distinction. And then they thought they'd have a better opportunity here.

speaker: SS
words: If he'd stayed in Germany, would that have really stopped him from rising in the social system?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, I don't know if you could rise in the social system there very much unless you're born to a social system there. I think that's the way it used to be.

speaker: SS
words: But here, nothing stopped him to do whatever he could?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Well, from the Mielke side, my grandfather that fought in the Civil War- now he was from the big landlords, his ancestors and he likedithis distinction either. That one person should be higher class than another.

speaker: SS
words: I thought you said he was lower. No, that was my other grandfather. Grandfather Meilke that fought in the Civil War, yes.

speaker: SS
words: The other one, he was from the higher station.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. My ancestors didnft like that distinction.

speaker: SS
words: That's interesting. Do you think that could be true of a lot of the people - other people that came to this community, too?

speaker: IMN
words: I kind of think so. I think so, although I do think that there isn't as much distinction now as there used to be. I don't know whether you found it that way or not- for richer people. Maybe not class distinction as much as, oh, financial.

speaker: SS
words: I think in a way you could say that's replaced the class distinc tion. It all depends if you have money then you're- that's good- I don't like that distinction either.

speaker: IMN
words: No. I don't think it's as bad as it used to be.

speaker: SS
words: I wouldn't know because I wasn't here!

speaker: IMN
words: No- because I know people that have lots and lots of money and they're just as common- just the same as everybody else.

speaker: SS
words: Was it like that in your community from the beginning? Was there much distinction?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I don't think so because at the beginning nobody had any finances. Everyone had- well, had very little.

speaker: SS
words: So you think people were kind of roughly equal?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, they were roughly equal.

speaker: SS
words: Were there some people that tried to put on airs anyway?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, once in a while. Once in a while there were some. Not very many.

speaker: SS
words: That would be kind of normal.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, it's normal. It's normal. About like every place else, I imagine. But they do help if there is any disaster they are just the most wonderful people to help you out in every way they can.

speaker: SS
words: In the early days was there much need to do that? To help each other or was it more each family for itself?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I think they helped each other out when they possibly could. Especially if there were any illnesses. I think they did. I think out in the country- it isn't so much, oh, everybody for themself as being kind of a big family, and if they needed help, why, they'd come and help you.

speaker: SS
words: Sickness; when you're talking about that- is there any times in parti cular that you are thinking of when there was sickness? I know, like the flu was pretty rough at that time.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. I know when our family had typhoid, nearly all the family had typhoid, my older brother was going to high school in Spokane and couldn't go and the other was going to Kendrick and had to out and my sister and I stayed out- we all had typhoid.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know where it came from?

speaker: IMN
words: From a water supply. My older brother was sewing sacks, he and another man was sewing sacks and they had f into some bad water. And, of course, those days if you had typhoid you were sick for thcee weeks. Didn't have any antibiotics or anything to-

speaker: SS
words: And you caught it?

speaker: IMN
words: Most of us- my father and one brother and grandmother didn't get it but all the rest of us did. And before that there were several families had it. And there was one family from Germany lived here, they lost their two boys; typhoid fever. Buried at the cemetery at.

speaker: SS
words: Was there fear in your family that not everybody would make it through that typhoid?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, - were hopeful that would. And one of my brothers died. He was a year and four months old and he got spinal meningitis and he died. And my uncle died.

speaker: SS
words: From typhoid?

speaker: IMN
words: From typhoid.

speaker: SS
words: At that same time?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, same time.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have neighbors help?

speaker: IMN
words: No, there were enough of us- my grandmother didn't have it and my dad didn't have it we could help each other. And, of course, you didn't want people to come in because you didn't want to give it to them.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember about it- do you remember being sick?

speaker: IMN
words: I remember it very, very well, because at that time all we got to eat was a little bit of- three tablespoons of broth with every bit of fat removed three times a day and that's what you got to eat for three weeks. And then when your fever was gone then you had to go back and start gradually with food. And you'd lost so much weight that there wasn't much left of you.

speaker: SS
words: I believe it.

speaker: IMN
words: Of course, that was the proper treatment at that time.

speaker: SS
words: Did your mother work hard?

speaker: IMN
words: Very, very hard. She worked very hard. She was the oldest one of her brothers and sisters and I think she was nineteen when she was married. There were ten children in the family and three of 'em passed away when they were children and there were seven left. And she was a wonderful, wonderful woman. And we all worked hard but we were all honest- taught to be honest. And we were all taught - well, my folks sent us all to school, There were very, very few children that even graduated from the eighth grade, and they sent us to high school and we also went to college. And my mother and dad always taught us, "better yourself, never think that you're better or above anybody else."

speaker: SS
words: That's interesting that they had those strong feelings. Why do you think they had those ideas at that time?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, I kind of think it was because my mother's mother, her folks were well educated, very well educated and belonged to the high class in Germany, and her father was of the poorer class and that's where she learned.class distinction; and she never liked it. "It isn't what you are born to or how much money you had, it's what you do with yourself." And she was very, very strong in teaching that to her children and so was my father.

speaker: SS
words: She probably liked the American system pretty well then since it's a Democracy instead of a-

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: inheritance and that kind of thing.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, that's right.

speaker: SS
words: But for honesty- was that something that you think was true of the whole- that they taught all the kids- that all the kids were taught at that time? This stress on honesty, or do you think that was special? In the family.

speaker: IMN
words: In our family?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: IMN
words: It was very special, I think. Although I find- think that most of the parents taught their children honesty.

speaker: SS
words: When they taught you honesty- was that really never to lie and that sort of thing?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, not to tell lies to hurt anyone. Your little white lies wouldn't hurt anything, but you know, to tell some lies that would be detri mental to someone else. Otherwise, what they called little white lies I suppose, if it would help anyone, that wouldn't hurt anything. And another thing we were always taught to think that we were more than any one else, because everyone had their station in life.

speaker: SS
words: Well, like the work your mother did; what were her responsibilities to the farm and to the family? That she carried out.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, see, to be good to one another and do our work honestly and fairly.

speaker: SS
words: Did she work outside very much? Did she help your father?

speaker: IMN
words: No, not too much. Well, probably milked cows and so something like that, She never went out in the field and worked. It's usually the older boys that did. And of course then, when they were big enough they went off to high school and to college and then the younger ones would work.

speaker: SS
words: Did your father have most of that land cleared when he got it, or did he clear it up when he got it after?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, it was only about half cleared. The country was new, that was in 1901 when my folks came from Minnesota and it was quite a new coun try and there was lots and lots of shrubs and brush and small trees and so he cleared up a lot, a lot of the land.

speaker: SS
words: Did he work out or was he able to get by right here?

speaker: IMN
words: No, just got by. at that time most of the people raised everything that they needed all except bread, the staples, bread and oh, a few things like that.

speaker: SS
words: What about say, like harvest time- did the men go outside to work?

speaker: IMN
words: No, the way they had the harvest done there used to be somebody had a threshing machine and an engine and then the farmers'd help one another with the wagons and horses, they'd haul the grain to the some pictures in that book about that. And they help each other out that way.

speaker: SS
words: It doesn't sound like there was much chance to get any hard cash.

speaker: IMN
words: There wasn't. There wasn't but very little hard cash. Because the only thing- well, you had some grain to sell, but you needed so much for your cattle and for your horses and for your hogs and chickens. And, of course, there was lots and lots of fruit trees. Had lots of big orchards there at that time. And I remember when I was just a little youngster that my dad sold peachplums one year, took 'em to Kendrick and I don't know, they shipped 'em out somewhere. And the way they picked 'em- they'd put 'em right in the big wagonbox and hauled 'em to Kendrick. Now everything's got to be wrapped and everything. And seems to me he sold some pears one year.

speaker: SS
words: To get a little money.

speaker: IMN
words: Just to get a little money.

speaker: SS
words: You say you got to go on to high school, was that at Kendrick?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I went to high school in Spokane. My two brothers had gradua ted from high school and so then I had an aunt living in Spokane and so I stayed with her and I went to high school there.

speaker: SS
words: What school?

speaker: IMN
words: Lewis and Clark.High Slchool. And then my brother as deputy assessor at Lewiston and then I went to normal school at Lewiston. I stayed with my brothers then.

speaker: SS
words: Did you learn teaching?

speaker: IMN
words: Yeah, I taught school.

speaker: SS
words: You taught afterwards?

speaker: IMN
words: Afterwards, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Where did you teach?

speaker: IMN
words: I taught Middle School-three miles from here and then I taught near Melrose.

speaker: SS
words: What was the first?

speaker: IMN
words: Middle School, about half way between here and Southwick. My son owns that farm now. And then the first year I taught, I taught at Melrose, near Gifford in the Meyer District.

speaker: SS
words: Well, did you get very good training to teach or did you learn it in the classroom?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, we learned. Of course, learning at school and actually teaching are two altogether different things. But you do have the fundamentals when you go. hd I liked it very, very much,and if I to do it over again, I think I'd like to do it again. Especially with little youngsters. Small children.

speaker: SS
words: What was it like when you were first teaching? I mean, what kind of experience was it?

speaker: IMN
words: I liked it very much, but I had a lot to learn, too! And what did I have, eleven pupils. I think I had six grades and some of 'em only had one in a grade, and we had to walk about a mile to school. And, I found it very, very interesting.

speaker: SS
words: Was it very demanding?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I don't think so. Well, you had to do all of your own work, you didn't have any-where they have a seat work up for child ren, you can buy it and have it all prepared, well, you had to pre pare everything yourself. And, I didn't find it demanding. And then there was one thing I always liked, is to be with the children out on their playground and play with them and see that it was all supervised.

speaker: SS
words: Did you find that each kid was an individual?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Very much so. Of course, the last year I taught school I had thtlty-three pupils and I had all eight grades, didn't give you much time for each class.

speaker: SS
words: Were there things that the kids really liked? You know, the things that were most popular with them, that you would do for 'em. The one thing that I knowAthey liked, and I really liked, too; on Friday afternoon after recess, we have, oh, just a little speech and I'd have the children tell some of their experiences, you know, and of course that would be so- it wasn't connected with their language but they could learn through it, and that was one of the most interestmg classes I think I ever had. And I don't know that they did or whether they imagined some of it! I know one of the boys used to tell such big storiesjand Ithink part of it was imagination. But

speaker: IMN
words: sometimes those people that have that imagination can get to be writers and so on.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like they were having a chance to express themselves.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: That's good. Was that your idea?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, that was my idea.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have much of a role to play in the communities that you taught in? Because I've heard that the teacher was a rather im portant person to the whole community.

speaker: IMN
words: No, not at that time. Maybe moreso, like Cameron was a little town, moreso, but really there wasn't. Of course, we had our pro grams and so on, but otherwise - it wasn't in the communities where I taught anyway. I think there were some communities.

speaker: SS
words: Were you able to keep the money you made from teaching, or did you use it to help the family?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, I didn't have to help the family. I did owe some, I had some debts when I started to school. The first year I taught, had a seven month school and got $60 a month, and I had some debts. My folks had loaned me so I could buy a watch, so I'd have something to keep time, and a few^odds and ends. I paid it back.

speaker: SS
words: When you were in Spokane and Lewiston did you work out at all at that time?

speaker: IMN
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: Or just straight go to school.

speaker: IMN
words: l Just straight went to school. I stayed with my aunt and uncle and helped take care of the children, they had four little children and I helped with the housework.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a public school? Lewis and Clark?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, it was one of the big high schools, at that time, Iguess the have lots of 'em in Spokane now. It was an exceptionally good school.

speaker: SS
words: And most of the people in the community didn't get a chance to do that, to go on.

speaker: IMN
words: No. No, I think our family and the Wagner family were about the only ones at that time. Of course now they all go to school, but at that time I think we were about the only ones. Well, my dad always said he could have had quite a few offers jobs in Minnesota, but he didn't have the schooling and so he says he was going to see to it that his children got to go to school. And so my mother and dad sacrificed a lot so we could go to school.

speaker: SS
words: In what way?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, they were poor, very poor and a lot of boys stayed home and they could buy big farms and everything. Of course my dad never had much land, they said, you children should be able to do something. And bohh my oldest brothers were attorneys.

speaker: SS
words: Where did they go to law school?

speaker: IMN
words: In Washington, D.C.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, really?

speaker: IMN
words: One of 'em went to- graduated from National Law College and the other one from George Washington.

speaker: SS
words: And did your parents foot the bill for that?

speaker: IMN
words: No, they worked their way through school.

speaker: SS
words: It seems like it would take a lot of drive to go all the way back there to go to school.

speaker: IMN
words: They had a lot of determination.

speaker: SS
words: From your parents? IMN Yes from my parents and my uncle, my mother's brother- said, MWliat. ever you do, go on to school." And then, one of my brothers was in Who's Who in the West. And my older brother was my teacher for two years.

speaker: SS
words: When you were in school? I was in school in Cameron. Said, My sisters.have to behave, if we didn't we'd get it when we got home." (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: Didn't want you to be the first to take advantage of him; right?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. And he modernized the schools. The teacher before-not the teacher before him-jjbut the girls sat on one side of the room and the boys on the other side, and then he'd come around and he'd have and he'd point a little pointer and he says, "Perfect or imperfect." And at re cess or at noon and when school was out and if you didn't whisper he said, "Perfect." If you whispered to someone said, "Imperfect." And if he said imperfect, you had to stay in.

speaker: SS
words: You had to say yourself?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. And then he'd go around and he'd ask everybody around if you whispered, and if they said, yes, if you didn't, he'd put down, "I told a lie." And if you told three lies, you'd get a spanking.

speaker: SS
words: Your brother cut that out?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. He cut it out and we had a wonderful school board. Mr. Blum was one of them, he had the blacksmith shop and he was very, very strong for school. And they had a new system for heating so it'd circulate and we didn't have to go to the corner and dip the dipper out and drink water out of it any more, that's what we hdd to do then wheijmy brother, they got afountain and each one had their own cup and we had lunch buckets - and a place to put all our lunch buckets instead of putting it in the back of the room and it'd freeze- and the lunch freezing. And, I don't know, there's so many- and had towels- had towels so we could wash and a wash basin. We never had any of that before.

speaker: SS
words: What about the chances of learning when you were going to school?

speaker: IMN
words: I think the learning was very good. Now the first teachers they had so many odd ways, but academically, learning, he did teach us things. We did learn. And then I think a lot of the students wan ted to, I know I did. I wanted to learn something. But we did learn. I think for early days the schools were adequate for that time. Course some schools may not have been. And then when my brother came to school, that's when we started having literary societies and school programs. Of Course he had gone on to high school in Spokane and he saw more modern ways.

speaker: SS
words: So those were innovations?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, they were all innovations. And the girls and boys were all put together, mixed up instead of just sitting the girls on one side and boys on the other.

speaker: SS
words: You say, this teacher before- where did he come from? Do you know what his background was? Because he does sound real oldfashioned.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, I don't know his background. He was oldfashioned in those ways and then in teaching right from the book: ; you did learn. One bad habit he did have- this is imperfect or perfect about telling lies- and then he'd always believe the other fellow.

speaker: SS
words: Not too good.

speaker: IMN
words: No. And then another thing that I never liked, he'd have an upper class and ask 'em a question and they couldn't answer then he'd ask the lower class, and if a lower classman could answer he'd make fun of the upper classman. And that's one thing I always vowed that when I taught school- that's one thing I absolutely would not do. And, I hope I never did.

speaker: SS
words: When did you decide that you were going to want to teach?

speaker: IMN
words: Ever since I was a little girl, I always wanted to teach school.

speaker: SS
words: Where do you think that came from? What made you decide?

speaker: IMN
words: My brother was a teacher, we've had teachers in our family and my great grandfather taught school in Germany for sixty-five years. That was a long, long time ago in the 1700's- 1800's.

speaker: SS
words: And I suppose that your mother- that your parents would have en couraged you to do that?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, they wanted us to go to school. I know my mother said she never got to go to school very much, she was the oldest one in the family and her mother was sick so much she had to stay home and take care of the children. And my dad said he could have had lots of jobs if had the schooling. And I wish you could see the books he kept, when he was the road overseer. And he had some office in the church where the bookkeeping was wonderful, for just having very, very lit tle school. Said when they went to school the teacher' put his feet on the desk, and he'd sleep and the kids tear around, and he said the boy that could wrestle the best was the best pupil. They knew what it meant to have some schooling.

speaker: SS
words: How much opportunity was there for mixing, between the young men and young women? I mean the chances to get to know each other and to kind of find yourself a partner?

speaker: INM
words: Well, here it was- we used to have- of course, there was the church and the school, we had dances. And then of course, when we got older where we could mix, had dances at Leland and ball games. Now Kendrick, we hardly ever got to Kendrick, about as far as Southwick and that was about the way they mixed.

speaker: SS
words: Well, say like a date: did they have dating?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh,yes, they had dating. Of course, the boy'd come with the buggy and horses. But I don't think they dated quite as young. I think they were a little bit older.

speaker: SS
words: To go on a date, where would you be likely to go?

speaker: IMN
words: Well,

speaker: SS
words: Would it be to someone else's house or something?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, to the dances and then to the literary societ , that q be about it. There weren't so many places to go to. But, of course, when I got older then at Kendrick they finally had the movies and that was about it.

speaker: SS
words: When did you meet Mr. Newman?

speaker: IMN
words: I've known him since we were children. We knew the family in Minnesota and we knew them here and then he went to Canada and Minnesota and then he came back here, and that's where I met him again.

speaker: SS
words: Had you been teaching already by the time-

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, then  Imet him again, he learned things by himself. Learned music and had an orchestra and he did taxidermy work. He learned that all by hisself, And, oh, so many, many things.

speaker: SS
words: Did he farm?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, yes he farmed.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have to stop teaching school when you got married?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, I quit teaching. And I had an opportunity to go back to teaching during World War II, when there was a shortage of teachers, and just the youngest son was at home, and so I asked my husband and my son- I had this opportunity to teach, you've got enough teaching right here at home!" So I never mentioned it again! (Chuckles) Well, I can see, too, I was needed here.

speaker: SS
words: I understood that really that women had to quit when they got married in most places.

speaker: IMN
words: They did when I was younger, yes, if they were married they couldn't teach.

speaker: SS
words: I wondered about that, I never understood too well. It seems like sort of a discrimination in a way.

speaker: IMN
words: it was. But that was- that they couldn't get married. Now, I could have taught- when I was married they could teach when they were married.

speaker: SS
words: So, you just chose not to?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have to do a lot of work yourself too to help out on the farm then after you were married?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, no. I never did any work on the farm,no. My husband's father lived with us and I never did work out. I never did work out in the field. I made my garden and took care of my garden and things like that, but not any field work.

speaker: SS
words: Did you milk cows?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, I milked cows.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think you did more or less what your mother had done?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Pretty much?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Of course, had it a little easier, little more modern things. Quite a bit. Washing, we had to do it by hand and so on until the electricity came. Of course, after a while we had the machines that we turned by hand, and really as far as easy is concerned, it wasn't easy until we could get the electricity.

speaker: SS
words: When did you get married? About what year?

speaker: SS
words: 1918. What was it like in the '20's for you and your husband farming? Were those rough years for you?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, we didn't have anything, but we just worked and just took it it came. See, we were just renting and had debts to pay off.

speaker: SS
words: How did the rental work? You lived in the house-

speaker: IMN
words: You lived in the house and then you paid one-third of the crop went to the owner and the renter had two-thirds, but of course the renter paid all the expenses. Well, that was just about right because the owner had taxes to pay and had to live, too.

speaker: SS
words: Did you find that you got ahead in the '20's quite a bit, or was it really just touch-and-go those years?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, a person never got ahead too far. Really, we didn't make any money- well, we finally bought this place and it took us a number of years to pay off the mortgage and then we bought another 200 acres, And of course, we didn't have it all paid off when my husband died. we sold it- when we sold it- we sold it to the family- kept it in the family.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I know that- people told me that when the Depression hit- and I don't know if it was true of you, but I've heard others say that when the Depression hit in the early '30's that it wasn't even much different than it had been because it had been so bad before.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, that's it. We never noticed it so much because we had a hard time before. Of Course, you didn't get a very good price for your things and so on, but you lived just about like you did before. You didn't buy any luxuries anyway, just the necessities.

speaker: SS
words: And that was the same as it had been for you in the '20's?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, yes. Just bought the necessities and worked hard. And, we were happy and satisfied.

speaker: SS
words: Well, now, I know that some of the- I mean the reputation of some of the people around here that have done awfully well as far as getting pretty good sized spreads and all. When did that develop for them? Was that the same time or was it later?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, now we had eighty acres and then we bought hundred acres, and we earned every penny of it. But a great many of the people around here had inherited. They had fathers- and then, of course, the children were home and worked and just got more and more a lot of it is inherited. The fathers- the parents had it.

speaker: SS
words: That's the same way as it is around Genesee.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, just practically the same thing.

speaker: SS
words: Those people that inherited from the parents had such a good start.

speaker: IMN
words: That's the way it is here. Well, see my dad didn't have so much as far as money is concerned because we all went on to school. Of course, my parents did pay for my schooling.

speaker: SS
words: And your husband's father- he didn't have a big spread either?

speaker: IMN
words: No, he had very little and he was a well educated man and he didn't have anything. He had absolutely nothing, he lost everything he had. Traded land here for some someplace in Washington and he lost that and went to Canada and when he went to Canada he lost everything he had.

speaker: SS
words: Where did he go in Canada? Do you know? Was it Alberta?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, Alberta, at Seven Persons, Alberta.

speaker: SS
words: So he was trying to farm in that prairie, in that northern country. Had a rough time, harvests and all.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: That's a common story. A lot of people that were-

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, I know. I should say. when

speaker: SS
words: Well, he traded his place here for land in eastern Washington.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, at Hay, Washington, it was in this desert country.

speaker: SS
words: Hay?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. And they say now that's a good farming country. See,they farm different now than they did at that time. And they say the people that live there now are doing real well.

speaker: SS
words: Did he come back here?

speaker: IMN
words: No, he went to Moscow and then from Moscow he went to Canada and Canada back to Minnesota and then they came back here.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I guess I'd like to ask you a little more about what the com munity around here itself was like when you were growing up. I would imagine it was bigger.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, it was much biggerMvery much bigger because there were more families and more children in the families. There used to be, oh, six, seven to ten children in a family. And I know at school at one time at Cameron there were as many as- that is in the grade school, there were as many as sixty in the school. And then, of course finally they had to quit school here because there weren't enough children to keep school.

speaker: SS
words: Then they started- that was consolidation.

speaker: IMN
words: That was consolidation with Kendrick.

speaker: SS
words: How did you feel about that? People feel about that? Were they very sorry to see the school go here?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, sorry in some ways and glad in another. Sorry to see the com munity life go out from school, because you had lots of activi ties in school and then kind of glad in another...

speaker: IMN
words: — I don't know how many schools there were and there was lots of friction there and jealousy. And when they consolidated everything goes down- and they all worked . the same thing. I think it was a better feeling all around in the consolidation, among the people.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of friction? You know, Just in- I don't mean any

speaker: IMN
words: Well, oh, maybe- this school is better and that school is better and then athletics- oh, they'd, of course, sometimes get just a little be yond what they should of good judgement. And now when they're all together, they all work toward the same thing.

speaker: SS
words: So, you mean that before every little community was kind of a rival with the others.

speaker: IMN
words: Rivals, that's right. Yes. And I think it gave 'em more opportunity although the little schools, I think, in their day were good, at that time, because otherwise the children wouldn't have had any schooling.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard- what I've heard against consolidation is that for some communities- it may not be true in Cameron- for some, the community kind of lost it's focus. Some of these little ones, because the school was the center.

speaker: IMN
words: I think that's the way it was with most of 'em. Because, that was the center and that's where you'd have your activities and different things and you don't have that now. And then, there is one thing about the smaller schools, all the students could take part in things. When they go to the bigger schools it's just the better ones that could take part in certain things. There areAsome things for it and somethings against it.

speaker: SS
words: What happened at Cameron? Do you think that the community kept itsself as a community after the schools consolidated, or do you think it lost something?

speaker: IMN
words: Maybe some things. Of course, there's one thing- they have the church here and they have activities there, so that kind of holds 'em together. And then they have 4H and that helpsA all this whole ter ritory. Of course it isn't at Cameron, it's at Leland but they all— but they come from all these places and I think that's one - that's another thing. And then we have, oh home demonstrations for the women. So there are a few things.

speaker: SS
words: It seems to me that the church was more important in Cameron than a lot of places from the beginning because I know a lot ofcoppmnities didn't get around to building a church until years after they had schools there and Cameron must have had a church almost as soon as they had a school.

speaker: IMN
words: Just about. Just about. And, because it happened to be- the minister happened to come here and get all congregation- the congregation getting people- and from the same religion, I think that's one reason that was for Cameron. And, of course, there were some other families, here, too, but a majority of 'em belonged to that.

speaker: SS
words: Were there any families in Cameron that werenit German at the time?

speaker: IMN
words: I think so, yes, there were some families here that weren't German.

speaker: SS
words: How much would they take part in the community life?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, not too much. AI remember- of course, it might have been more before- you know, before I came here- and I was a little youngster, that I don't remember.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder if they learned how to talk German, these people who were not German?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I don't think so.

speaker: SS
words: Say, like in school- did you go to school with kids that weren't Ger man?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, yes, yes. And there is one thing I know that my sister and I well, a lot of times all get together and talk German and there'd be some of them that couldn't talk German. Well, we always talked English because we always felt that it wasn't very nice for those that couldn't understand, they'd think we were talking about 'em. We always tried to make 'em welcome. Of course, we were always taught that home and anybody came to our house, my mother always said , didn't make a difference who they were, "You treat them nice. You don't have to associate with people that you don't want to, but if they come to your home, you be civil to 'em."

speaker: SS
words: How much of this visiting, coming into homes really was there?

speaker: IMN
words: There was a lot of it. There was just a lot of it. People would go from one home to another on Sunday.

speaker: SS
words: Every Sunday?

speaker: IMN
words: No, not every Sunday, but quite a few Sundays. And then it was justthey wouldn't have a great, big dinner just eat what they haolt . (I hope I can help you with something else. I don't know if I've helped you or not)

speaker: SS
words: (oh, you have- because it's really what we want to try to save as much as we can, what life was like from people and similarities.)

speaker: IMN
words: In the early days they used to have baseball games, I can remember. If it was on Sunday they'd meet and have baseball and horseshoe pit ching. Used to have a lot of horseshoe pitching.

speaker: SS
words: Horseshoe pitching? This would be a community affair?

speaker: IMN
words: No, it wouldn't be community, just when they'd visit. And some times, too, if they'd have these picniss and so on. I'm trying to think of some other things.

speaker: SS
words: I was thinking about asking about the kind of opportunities that girls had compared to boys in those days.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, I don't know, not too much I think. The only things the girls could do was go out and do some housework or something. And they didn't have the opportunities that have now.

speaker: SS
words: That was common, housework. Did you do any of that?

speaker: IMN
words: No. My sister and I always told my parents that we weren't going to be anybody's slave. We'd go out and dig ditches before we'd go be somebody's slave! We just absolutely going to do that.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder why.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, we wanted to go on to school and learn something and do some thing with ourselves.

speaker: SS
words: Did many of your friends work as maids? I know Hattie Johnson did.

speaker: IMN
words: A few. There were some, yes, yes. Well, when ww were youngsters, we didn't look at it the way we should have, we didn't want to do it be- cause- and those people are just as honest as anybody, but we didn't want to do it. Well, to this day, I don't really like housework!! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: But it seems like with the choices that were open to women at that time, what else could they do?

speaker: IMN
words: There was nothing could do, there were no opportunities. Once in a while some girls would work in a store, but there weren't many stores, either. There just weren't any opportunities for children.

speaker: SS
words: Well,now, I've heard a lot of talk about the old counntry ways com pared to the new country, and that in the old country that the women were looked upon more as slaves, more as just taking the orders and the husbands making the decisions. Would you say that that's true difference between the old and the new country?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, I think so. I think that as time went on the women had more to say, although in our household my mother had just as much, say as Dad.

speaker: SS
words: Really, ever since you were kids?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, can remember.

speaker: SS
words: They shared the responsibility of decisions?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Made decisions together?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: They shared the responsibility of decisions?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: He didn't just make 'em for her?

speaker: IMN
words: Uh-huh. No,they were made together. If they were made separately the other one heard about it, they worked together.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that was a little unusual?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, more so. I think so. I think at first the men did have more. The woman was supposed to be in the house and do the housework and the man was supposed to be the boss. I think that was quite a bit of the attitude.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think your father minded to have his wife making decisions?

speaker: IMN
words: No, no, no. No, he was was very well satisfied.

speaker: SS
words: That's good.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, worship,;my mother.

speaker: SS
words: Well, that's good because I had the idea that it was kind of dif ferent and that's one of the reasons- I don't know if it's true, but it seemed like one of the reasons that girls didn't have the oppor tunities, was because of that old idea.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, and then, well, you know, they didn't go to school and things or go and look out for work they couldn't get away from it, they couldn't get very far and do something else. Weren't many opportunities for girls at that time.

speaker: SS
words: In your family, did they treat the girls differently than the boys as far as what you could and couldn't do?

speaker: IMN
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: The boys didn't have more independence than the girls?

speaker: IMN
words: No, no more. No, that was the same.

speaker: SS
words: That's different, too, than a lot of families, because I've heard a lot of people tell me that the girls just didn't have the freedom that their brothers did.

speaker: IMN
words: No, we did. We did, one was like the other.

speaker: SS
words: What about your friends- girl friends?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, I don't know. You know when you're young like that you don't pay too much attention. But it seems to me that it was just about the same at most places, like it was at home. " I can remember, I think most of the people treated their boys and girls alike. Because I know when I went on to high school that my dad was telling me, "Why in the world do you send the girls to school?" Says, "Girls don't need to go to school." And there were a few, quite a few of 'em said they wished they could send theirs.

speaker: SS
words: Quite a few?

speaker: IMN
words: Quite a few the people that wished they could send their girls to school.

speaker: SS
words: But others said- why-

speaker: IMN
words: Why do you send 'em, because girls will never do anything but get married anyway. "Well," my dad said, "even if they do get married they could use it in their married life just the same as anywhere else."

speaker: SS
words: Well, do you think that's it's better now, as far as the opportunfties that women have?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, yes. Much, much better. Women have opportunities to get in most any kind of work now and they couldn't years ago.

speaker: SS
words: If you could have had more of a career, would you have wanted to, do you think?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I think I'd still want to go to teaching. I liked teaching, especially the little fellas. The little children. I still love the little children now.

speaker: SS
words: But still you felt that you had to quit when you got married, and nowdays, of course, there's women—

speaker: IMN
words: No, well, years ago, had to go away from home to teach, because we didn't have cars in those days and if a person could get back and forth it would get back and forth, it'd probably be different. And now, the life is altogether different now. Maybe some of it is for the better and some of it isn't!so good.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think wouldn't be as good now as it used to be?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, maybe too much freedom, I don't know, it depends upon the individual, I think.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there's as much neighboring?

speaker: IMN
words: No, not near as much. -'No, because, well, one thing is television- people stay home for that. And you have the car and opportunities to go fatther away from home, and probably have to go quite a ways to get certain things that are really worthwhile, concerts and different things and to learn and to enjoy themselves.

speaker: SS
words: When you were married did you and your husband share responsibilities too. decisions?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, we always did. We always did. 'Course, we didn't always agree but we could come to an agreement by talking things over.

speaker: SS
words: Did you go together for very long? Was there court ships in those days?

speaker: IMN
words: Yeah, we went together for about a year, a year and a little over. Of course, I had known him all my life.

speaker: SS
words: Were you married in the church here?

speaker: IMN
words: No, we were married at Lewiston.

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you about play when you were a kid, what sorts of things you did- you know, what girls' play was.

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, let's see, of course, we always played with dolls. Do you mean when we got older or when we was just little?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, both, really.

speaker: IMN
words: When we got older? Oh, we'd do some fancywork and we played the Ouija board and we played cards and play

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, that's a big board and checker board and things like that. And we'd play ball. We played a lot of baseball.

speaker: SS
words: Girls, too?

speaker: IMN
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: With the boys or with the girls?

speaker: IMN
words: With the boys. I played with the boysAin the eighth grade, didn't have enough boys so I had to play with 'em, I and my girl friend. And my oJuter brother- my brothers played on baseball teams and I'd have to play with 'em. My brother next to me, I'd always have to for him- pitch for him. And I loved baseball anyway.

speaker: SS
words: Would that be like Cameron and Leland?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was thelte- hot rivalry?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, quite a bit. Quite a bit.

speaker: SS
words: and Juliaetta really had a hot one.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, quite a bit. I don't think it was quite as bad as Kendrick and Juliaetta. Of course, maybe there wasn t quite as many people, young people. And that's the way it used to be with the schools, too. Basketball games. There used to be very much of a rivalry. I know because my youngsters all played.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have the play party games as part of the literaries and the school doings?

speaker: IMN
words: Play? You mean, have plays?

speaker: SS
words: Play parties, I was thinking- the games like the Miller Boy.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, we used to play that and oh, Spin the Bottle.

speaker: SS
words: Spin the bottle?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, and, oh, Skip to My Lou, and, oh-

speaker: SS
words: Was Spin the Bottle a kissing game?

speaker: IMN
words: No, you spin the bottle and you got the question and point to 'em and you'd do that and then they'd have to ask a question.

speaker: SS
words: Were there forfeits?

speaker: IMN
words: No, not with Spin the Bottle, but with some of 'em we played forfeits. Used to play, I'm trying to think of some things- I've kind of forgotten- It's been quite a while since we played- and oh, and, Telephone was another one, we played. Did you ever play it?

speaker: SS
words: No, how?

speaker: IMN
words: One would start whispering and they'd have to pass it on and we al ways tried to be careful so that there wasn't three or four boys sitting next to each other, because by the time it reached the next person it was something altogether different than it should be!

speaker: SS
words: You girls kept it straight better?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. They'd keep it straight. And see, what else did we play? Um- Cross Questions and Silly Answers.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard of that, now how did that work?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, they'd line up boys on one side and the girls on the ohter and someone'd give this side some questions and thettone opposite would have to answer it. And that side, someone would go around and give 'em an answer and they asked on this side and answered on that, or whatever, you'd get some crazy answers.

speaker: SS
words: Well, now, would each side work out an answer together.

speaker: IMN
words: No,hone person'd have to go on each side and give the questions and answers. They used to have a lot of fun playing games. I can't re member all of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: I can understand it, it's been a long time.

speaker: IMN
words: Years ago.

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, it's been sixty years ago.

speaker: SS
words: Now what occasions would you play those games on? In a literary or-?

speaker: IMN
words: No. That'd be when young people'd meet at different homes. They'd meet at different homes and then they'd have those games. At literaries, that's where we'd have our debates and our paper and our singing and little plays and different acts.

speaker: SS
words: Were any of the games kissing games, at that time?

speaker: IMN
words: Forfeit sometimes would be kissing, but there were some but not too many. But there some. Oh, the one that was a kissing game was Post Office. (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: How did they play Post Office then?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, let's see, maybe a couple had to go into a room and someone would have to be the doorkeeper and the couple in the would have to name somebody to come in that room and then the boy'd have to come and get the girl, to see to it that he got the right girl, and so many kisses. I remember that now.

speaker: SS
words: Well, were they taking turns, in other words, a boy would call a girl in and a girl would call a boy in?

speaker: IMN
words: No- or was it that way? Now, that could have been it, too.

speaker: IMN
words: Now, it could have been that way, too, maybe it was that way. It's been so long ago that I don't remember whether- huh- I wouldn't doubt that that's the way it was- when did the right girl come-

speaker: SS
words: Did you get to pick who you wanted to come in; did you know who was going to come in?

speaker: IMN
words: Idon't remember that. Ijustldon't really remember. Ithought^the boy had to pick a girl and this couple would have to come in, but I forget- I think the way you say must to have been it. To really tell the truth. I think you're right.

speaker: IMN
words: I think the boy- so many stamps for some one, until the right fick her boy. girl would come, and then she'd go in hart Whatever boy she wanted for so many stamps.

speaker: SS
words: I know there was a lot of different ways they played that game. Well, did you have any kind of game like Skip-To-My-Lou played at school?

speaker: IMN
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: This was all homes?

speaker: IMN
words: This was homes. At school we played Blackman. Pull-Away, I think you know that.

speaker: SS
words: That's my name for Blackman. Is that a catch game? Like catch a kid?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, from one base to another. And then we used to play- fthey used to Crack-the-Whip.

speaker: SS
words: How did you do that?

speaker: IMN
words: Have a whole, big- lined up, you know, take ahold of hands and then the leader, he'd run and pull the string around and the one at the tale end- was sent flying, just break your bones.

speaker: SS
words: These were outdoor games?

speaker: IMN
words: outdoor eames.

speaker: SS
words: These were played in the schoolyard?

speaker: IMN
words: Yeah. And we used to play- huh- Dare Base; sides. And how in the world was that? There were two sides- oh, gee, I've forgot how that was played.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard of that game though.

speaker: IMN
words: I don't even remember how it was played. I haven't seen it played for sixty years. Oh, gracious. We used to play a lot of baseball.

speaker: SS
words: Was that the most popular game?

speaker: IMN
words: I think so.

speaker: SS
words: When you were teaching?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, we used to play baseball.

speaker: SS
words: Well, did the older kids prefer a game like baseball to a game like Blackman?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: It did it matter in the age?

speaker: IMN
words: No, it didn't make any difference, we played- the little ones and the big ones all played baseball, and I'd be there with them. And then we played Dare Base- but I've forgotten how it was played. And we used to play Run-Sheep-Run. Gracious, how in the world was that played?

speaker: SS
words: I've heard of that game.

speaker: IMN
words: I don't even remember how that was played. And, "Old Mother Pigeon Toe, lost her needle and could not sew." But I don't remember how it was played. I remember the words.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know the name of the game?

speaker: IMN
words: It's Old Mother Pigeon Toe.

speaker: SS
words: I never heard of that one. That's a new one to me.

speaker: IMN
words: I don't remember that one.

speaker: SS
words: Well, let me ask you about threshing-

speaker: IMN
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: -was that-a big deal at that time?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, very big. And the threshing machine would come and thresh the grain. And it used to be the threshing crew;Kone to thirty people. And of course then, they'd bring their teams and their horses and they'd haul the grain to the threshing machine on these racks. And the women they had breakfast about five o'clock, and then they'd have lunch-they'd fix a lunch; coffee and sandwiches and cookies at nine o'clock in the morning and then they'd have a big dinner at noon^meat and potatoes and vegetables and pies. And the only thing to drink at that time was coffee, tea or lemonade, there was nothing else but lemonade for soft drinks. And then they'd have lunch in the afternoon at three-thirty of coffee and sandwiches and cookies and then we'd have a dinner at night about seven o'clock.

speaker: SS
words: Each at each home? This was when they were at your place?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. That's the way it was in this part of the country. Now some places, the threshing machines had a cookwagon, and they had a regular cook and they served the meals in there, but this territory around here, as long as I can remember, they cooked in the home.

speaker: SS
words: Why do you think the difference- why one over the other?

speaker: IMN
words: I just have no idea.

speaker: SS
words: How long would they be at a place?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, it depended upon how much you had to thresh. Sometimes a day, sometimes two days, and if it rained, sometimes three four days when the threshing machies came from a distance. If it was just the neighbors then they could go home, but for a while the threshing crews came, oh, maybe ten, fifteen miles away. Of course they couldn't go home.

speaker: SS
words: There was no threshing machine right in the neighborhood then?

speaker: IMN
words: Not at first. After a while there was, but I know my husband had a threshing machine-

speaker: SS
words: Did he own it with other men?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, with others.

speaker: SS
words: And they did the whole area?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, quite a few of us. And then after the threshing machine was gone they had the combine.

speaker: SS
words: When you did the cooking, would neighbor ladies oome and help, or waa it just done-

speaker: IMN
words: Well, you'd do it yourself, if you needed help you got help from some of the neighbors or some of the young girls would come and help you.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a very exciting time when you were young?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, for the young people it was a lot of fun. There'd always be young boys at the threshing machine, or at least they hoped there would be! That was^the grain threshing and then after that there was bean threshing and a different type of grain- changed and the different styles changed.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of chores were you expected to do when you were a young girl?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, had to get in wood. Had to do dishes. Help hoe in the garden. Help take care of the children- the smaller children. Gather eggs; feed calves and then when we got a little older we had to milk the cows and gather in some of the fruits, and so on. Dig potatoes. And so many children had to stay home and do so much work. Nc , our family, the only things we ever had to stay home for is to help dig the potatoes. And I hate to dig a potato to this day!(Chuckles) And my sister and I, we cried, we didn't want to stay home, we thought maybe that would help so we didn't have to stay out of school, We just loved school, but that crying didn't help. We had to dig po tatoes anyway!

speaker: SS
words: What did families elsewhere- I've heard that the boys, like, stayed out of school more than they went helping at home? Was that true here, too?

speaker: IMN
words: Not in my family, because we all liked to go to school.

speaker: SS
words: But, I mean others.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, some of 'em probably. Once in a while some boys would play hookey!

speaker: SS
words: I wasn't thinking so much of hookey as to-

speaker: IMN
words: -to stay home and work.

speaker: SS
words: Work, yeah;

speaker: IMN
words: Well, A LOT OF BOYS, I don't know if they wanted to stay home or if they had to stay home and work.

speaker: SS
words: Well, this potato digging, did that take very long?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, one day, but we didn't want to stay out one day. No, we al ways wanted to learn something. Well, we were always taught that from home, too, to learn and try to better ourselves.

speaker: SS
words: And you had a lot of opportunity in the school to do it, even though it was just a one-room school and just had one teacher for all the grades?

speaker: SS
words: Was there much homework to do then?

speaker: IMN
